text.title: GKE and Cloud Run Best Practices
text.summary: GKE Autopilot vs Standard decision, node pool patterns, Cloud Run configuration, and Spot VM usage.

rule.statement: Use GKE Autopilot for new workloads unless they require GPU, privileged containers, custom node configs, or Windows nodes — Autopilot covers 80%+ of use cases with less operational overhead.
rule.rationale: Autopilot manages node provisioning, OS patching, and kubelet config. Standard mode requires manual node pool sizing, OS upgrades, and kubelet tuning. The operational difference is 20-40 hours/month for a team managing Standard clusters.

list.item: Autopilot: pay-per-pod resources, automatic node provisioning, no OS patching, no node pool management
Standard: GPU workloads, privileged containers, custom machine types, Windows containers
Hybrid (2025): Standard cluster with Autopilot workload profiles — for teams migrating

rule.statement: Use Spot VMs for batch jobs, CI/CD runners, and non-critical background workers — they save 60-91% over on-demand with the only trade-off being preemption.
rule.rationale: Spot VMs are reclaimed by GCP with a 30-second notice when capacity is needed. This is acceptable for stateless batch workloads that can be retried. The cost savings make CI/CD on GKE economically viable vs managed CI services.

example.code: # Node pool for batch/CI workloads node_pools: - name: spot-batch machine_type: n2-standard-8 spot: true taints: - key: workload value: batch effect: NoSchedule autoscaling: min_node_count: 0 max_node_count: 20
example.note: Use tolerations in batch job pods to schedule onto spot nodes. Non-batch pods without the toleration automatically land on on-demand nodes.

rule.statement: Set min-instances to 0 for cost-sensitive services and to 1+ for latency-sensitive services — cold starts on Cloud Run take 200ms-2s depending on image size.
rule.rationale: min-instances=0 means the service sleeps when idle, saving cost but adding cold start latency on first request. min-instances=1 keeps one warm instance, adding ~$5-20/month but eliminating cold starts.

rule.statement: Set concurrency to 80-100 for typical web apps; lower (1-10) for CPU-intensive or stateful per-request work.
rule.rationale: Cloud Run bills per-CPU-second. High concurrency means fewer instances needed for the same throughput. But CPU-bound or stateful workloads need isolation per request or they contend for CPU within the instance.

example.code:
# Blue-green deploy with gradual traffic shift gcloud run deploy my-service \ --image=gcr.io/project/app:v2 \ --no-traffic \ --tag=green
# Validate green tag directly curl https://green---my-service-xxx.run.app/healthz
# Gradual rollout: 10% to new version gcloud run services update-traffic my-service \ --to-tags=green=10,latest=90
# Full cutover gcloud run services update-traffic my-service \ --to-latest
